elements.sent-2411-001: Large Language Models (LLMs) are prone to off-topic misuse, where users may prompt these models to perform tasks beyond their intended scope.
elements.sent-2411-002: Current guardrails, which often rely on curated examples or custom classifiers, suffer from high false-positive rates, limited adaptability, and the impracticality of requiring real-world data that isn't available in pre-production.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we introduce a flexible, data-free guardrail development methodology that addresses these challenges.
elements.sent-2411-004: By thoroughly defining the problem space qualitatively and passing this to an LLM to generate diverse prompts, we construct a synthetic dataset to benchmark and train off-topic guardrails that outperform heuristic approaches.
elements.sent-2411-005: Additionally, by framing the task as classifying whether the user prompt is relevant with respect to the system prompt, our guardrails effectively generalize to other misuse categories, including jailbreak and harmful prompts.
elements.sent-2411-006: Lastly, we further contribute to the field by open-sourcing both the synthetic dataset\footnote{\href{https://huggingface.co/datasets/gabrielchua/off-topic {https://huggingface.co/datasets/gabrielchua/off-topic and the off-topic guardrail models\footnote{\href{https://huggingface.co/collections/govtech/off-topic-guardrail-673838a62e4c661f248e81a4 {https://huggingface.co/collections/govtech/off-topic-guardrail-673838a62e4c661f248e81a4 , providing valuable resources for developing guardrails in pre-production environments and supporting future research and development in LLM safety.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4o \cite{openai2024gpt4ocard , Gemini 1.5 \cite{geminiteam2024gemini15unlockingmultimodal , and Llama 3 \cite{dubey2024llama3herdmodels have revolutionised various sectors by enabling advanced natural language processing capabilities.}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Their applications extend beyond conversational agents to include tasks like documentation extraction, report generation, and workflow automation \cite{10.1145/3613905.3650841 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: As these models become increasingly integrated into software applications and real-world processes, ensuring their appropriate use becomes critically important.
elements.sent-2411-010: To mitigate potential risks associated with LLM deployments, significant efforts have been made in developing safety measures like model alignment \cite{NIPS2017_d5e2c0ad and guardrails \cite{dong2024safeguardinglargelanguagemodels .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Alignment techniques aim to ensure that LLMs behave in accordance with human values and intentions, while guardrails are mechanisms that prevent models from generating undesired or harmful outputs.
elements.sent-2411-012: These safety measures are essential for maintaining user trust and meeting regulatory compliance, especially in sensitive domains such as healthcare, finance, and legal services.
elements.sent-2411-013: One significant challenge is preventing LLMs from responding to prompts that fall outside their intended scope, typically defined by the system prompts set by developers.
elements.sent-2411-014: While prompted to perform specific tasks, LLMs are susceptible to producing responses to irrelevant or unintended prompts.
elements.sent-2411-015: For instance, one could get a healthcare policy chat bot to generate Python code with minimal prompting.
elements.sent-2411-016: We refer to such prompts as "off-topic" (see Figure~\ref{fig:example_off_topic ) which are distinct from "jailbreak" prompts \cite{shen2024donowcharacterizingevaluating which aim to elicit harmful or explicitly disallowed content.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Off-topic prompts may lead to benign outputs, but can still undermine the model's intended functionality and pose compliance risks.
elements.sent-2411-018: For example, providing legal or medical advice that is beyond the intended scope.
elements.sent-2411-019: Current guardrail mechanisms often rely on curated examples of positive/negative prompts \cite{amazonBlockDenied or custom classifiers \cite{microsoftCustomCategories for each use-case and trained on existing datasets to filter out inappropriate inputs.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, real-world user data is not yet available in pre-production, and there is typically a lack of sufficient data or examples at the early stages to a cover the vast array of potential off-topic prompts.
elements.sent-2411-021: Other guardrail implementations require defining a blacklist of topics \cite{rebedea2023nemoguardrailstoolkitcontrollable which can be challenging in pre-production as there are potentially infinite number of edge cases to consider.}
elements.sent-2411-022: This situation highlights three major challenges: first, the need for a general-purpose model that can effectively detect off-topic prompts, second, developing such a general-purpose model without extensive pre-existing datasets, and third, such real-world data is absent in the pre-production phase.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{example.png} \caption{Example of on- and off-topic user prompts: The goal is to correctly classify if a prompt is off-topic or not, with respect to the system prompt} \label{fig:example_off_topic}
elements.sent-2411-023: In this paper, we consider these challenges and introduce a flexible, data-free guardrail development methodology and apply it to the challenge of off-topic prompt detection for LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-024: By thoroughly defining the problem space qualitatively and leveraging an LLM to generate a diverse set of prompts, we construct a synthetic dataset that serves both as a benchmark and a training resource for off-topic guardrails.
elements.sent-2411-025: Fine-tuning embedding or cross-encoder models on this synthetic data outperforms heuristic approaches by reducing false positives, and enhancing potential adoption.
elements.sent-2411-026: Additionally, we find that by framing this as classifying whether the user prompt is relevant to the system prompt, our guardrail generalises effectively to other misuse categories, including jailbreak and harmful prompts.
elements.sent-2411-027: To summarize, our contributions are fourfold:.
elements.sent-2411-028: A Flexible Guardrail Development Methodology: We propose a scalable approach to generate synthetic datasets in pre-production to develop guardrails, thus providing a strong baseline for the LLM application's initial deployment.
elements.sent-2411-029: Performant Off-Topic Guardrails: We develop simple yet effective classifier guardrails by fine-tuning embedding and cross-encoder models on synthetic data.
elements.sent-2411-030: These models outperform existing heuristic methods, significantly reducing false positives and improving detection accuracy for off-topic prompts.
elements.sent-2411-031: Generalization to Multiple Misuse Categories: By framing the detection task in terms of system prompt relevance, our methodology effectively extends to other misuse types.
elements.sent-2411-032: Open-Source Resources: We contribute to the community by open-sourcing both the synthetic dataset and the off-topic guardrail models, facilitating future research and development in LLM safety and compliance.
elements.sent-2411-033: Ensuring that LLMs behave in accordance with human values and intentions is a critical area of research.
elements.sent-2411-034: Alignment techniques aim to steer the model's outputs towards desired behaviors while minimising harmful or unintended responses.
elements.sent-2411-035: Methods such as Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) \cite{NIPS2017_d5e2c0ad, 10.5555/3600270.3602281 or preference optimisation approaches \cite{10.5555/3666122.3668460, lu2024discoveringpreferenceoptimizationalgorithms have been employed to fine-tune models based on human preferences.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Prior work has also explored establishing a hierarchy of instructions \cite{wallace2024instructionhierarchytrainingllms , where the system prompt is given higher priority over user prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-037: This hierarchical approach ensures that the model adheres to its intended functionality, even when faced with off-topic user prompts.
elements.sent-2411-038: However, these approaches may also inadvertently reduce the helpfulness of the model (i.e., over refusals or false positives) \cite{ganguli2022redteaminglanguagemodels .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Guardrails are another mechanism designed to prevent LLMs from generating undesired or harmful outputs \cite{inan2023llamaguardllmbasedinputoutput, 10.1609/aaai.v37i12.26752, bonaldi-etal-2024-safer .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Where alignment is about model-level interventions, we define guardrails here as separate filters that scan the inputs and outputs of the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-041: Such guardrails can be updated or replaced independently of the main model, offering flexibility and adaptability to new types of misuse.
elements.sent-2411-042: External filters may use rule-based systems, machine learning classifiers, or a combination of both to detect and mitigate inappropriate content.
elements.sent-2411-043: Several frameworks and services have been developed to facilitate the implementation of guardrails, including NVIDIA NeMo Guardrails \cite{rebedea2023nemoguardrailstoolkitcontrollable , AWS Bedrock Guardrails \cite{amazonBlockDenied , Azure AI Content Safety \cite{microsoftCustomCategories .}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Synthetic data generation has emerged as a valuable technique for training and evaluating machine learning models, particularly when real-world data is scarce, sensitive, or difficult to collect \cite{liu2024bestpracticeslessonslearned .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Previous work has explored the effectiveness of synthetic data in training models for various tasks such as automatic labelling \cite{long2024llmsdrivensyntheticdatageneration , Q\&A/Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) evaluation \cite{xu2024simragselfimprovingretrievalaugmentedgeneration , instructional dialogue generation \cite{wang2023selfinstructaligninglanguagemodels , or for LLM agents \cite{mitra2024agentinstructgenerativeteachingagentic .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Other work has also explored generating diverse synthetic data with minimal or no human intervention \cite{xu2024magpiealignmentdatasynthesis .}
elements.sent-2411-047: In this section, we present a general framework for developing guardrails for LLMs without relying on pre-existing datasets.
elements.sent-2411-048: We then apply this framework to the specific problem of off-topic prompt detection.
elements.sent-2411-049: Developing effective guardrails for LLMs is crucial to ensure they operate within their intended scope and to prevent misuse.
elements.sent-2411-050: Some guardrail approaches depend on curated datasets of misuse examples, which are impractical due to the infinite variety of potential misuse scenarios, and due to the lack of data in pre-production.
elements.sent-2411-051: To address this challenge, we propose a flexible, data-free guardrail development framework (see Figure~\ref{fig:overview ) comprising the following steps:.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Qualitative Problem Analysis and Edge Case Identification: We begin by thoroughly analysing the specific misuse or safety issue that needs to be addressed.
elements.sent-2411-053: This involves understanding the model's intended functionality and identifying potential misuse or undesirable behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-054: By exploring potential misuse cases and edge scenarios, we can qualitatively define the boundaries of acceptable and unacceptable inputs and outputs.
elements.sent-2411-055: Synthetic Data Generation via LLM Prompting: Having described the problem space and the attributes of desired and undesired inputs/outputs, we leverage an LLM to generate synthetic data.
elements.sent-2411-056: We provide detailed prompts to the LLM that outline the types of acceptable and unacceptable interactions.
elements.sent-2411-057: To ensure diversity in the synthetic dataset, we can increase the model’s generation temperature and provide random seed words.
elements.sent-2411-058: To ensure the model outputs fit within the defined schema (e.g., for every scenario, generate five positive and five negative examples), we can employ structured outputs or constrained generation, which is offered by several LLM API providers.
elements.sent-2411-059: Additionally, including few-shot examples can enhance realism in the outputs.
elements.sent-2411-060: Model Training: With the synthetic dataset prepared, we proceed to train a classifier to detect undesirable inputs.
elements.sent-2411-061: As the problem is now effectively a text classification problem, transformer-based models are well-suited for this task.
elements.sent-2411-062: The benefits of this framework include:.
elements.sent-2411-063: Pre-Deployment Readiness: We can now build a classifier before deploying the application, ensuring a high baseline of safety measures are in place from the outset.
elements.sent-2411-064: Once the application has been deployed, real-world prompts can be collected to augment the synthetic dataset and further train and refine the model.
elements.sent-2411-065: Probability Scoring: The classifier model can provide probability scores for predictions, allowing for threshold-based decisions and nuanced handling of inputs.
elements.sent-2411-066: For example, developers may choose not to block a text outright but instead modify it or provide warning labels if the probability scores fall within a medium range.
elements.sent-2411-067: We apply this framework to the specific case of detecting off-topic prompts in LLM interactions.
elements.sent-2411-068: Given a system prompt ($S$) and a user prompt ($U$), we want to detect if the prompt is off-topic ($Y=1$) or on-topic ($Y=0$).
elements.sent-2411-069: This is thus a binary classification problem, where we seek to find the optimal function $F(.)$ that maximizes the classification metric of interest (e.g., PR-AUC, F1). \begin{equation}F(S,U) \in {0,1}\end{equation} Here, we define any prompt that is not relevant to the system prompt as being off-topic.
elements.sent-2411-070: With this definition, the system prompt should typically define a well-defined, closed-ended task.
elements.sent-2411-071: To generate the synthetic dataset, we used an LLM to create diverse examples of system prompts and corresponding on-topic and off-topic within the same LLM generation.
elements.sent-2411-072: We used GPT 4o 2024-08-06 and its structured outputs feature \cite{StructuredOutputs2024 to generate over 2M system and user prompt pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-073: We ensured diversity by varying the length of the prompts, providing random seed words, and randomising the real-life examples of system prompts.
elements.sent-2411-074: This synthetic dataset serves both as a training set for our classifier and as a benchmark for evaluating different guardrail approaches.
elements.sent-2411-075: This dataset, along with the generation prompt, has been open-sourced.
elements.sent-2411-076: One highlight of this dataset is its aim to reflect real-world enterprise applications of LLMs (e.g., report generation, Q\&A, and document extraction) as much as possible.
elements.sent-2411-077: Some benchmarks test a guardrail’s ability to prevent leakage defined in the system prompts \cite{toyer2024tensor .}
elements.sent-2411-078: We find such examples to not fully reflect the enterprise applications of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-079: We experimented with two modeling approaches for the off-topic detection problem (see Figure~\ref{fig:modelling ).}
elements.sent-2411-080: For the purposes of our experiments, we did not conduct hyperparameter optimisation.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{architecture.png} \caption{Summary of the two modelling approaches for the off-topic prompt detection} \label{fig:modelling}
elements.sent-2411-082: We started with a pre-trained embedding model that is lightweight and supports a long context length.
elements.sent-2411-083: Specifically, we used the jina-embeddings-v2-small-en model \cite{günther2024jinaembeddings28192token}\footnote{\href{https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-embeddings-v2-small-en {https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-embeddings-v2-small-en , which has 33 million parameters and supports a sequence length of up to 8192 tokens.}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: For our experiment, we limited the sequence length to 1024 tokens.
elements.sent-2411-085: The architecture involves feeding the system prompt and user prompt separately into the embedding model and training an adapter layer for each input.
elements.sent-2411-086: We introduced cross-attention layers so that the system prompt can attend to the user prompt, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-087: After obtaining the representations, we applied attention pooling to derive single vector representations for both prompts.
elements.sent-2411-088: These vectors were concatenated and passed through a classification head to make the final on-topic or off-topic prediction.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-090: We also fine-tuned pre-trained cross-encoder models, specifically cross-encoder/stsb-roberta-base \footnote{\href{https://huggingface.co/cross-encoder/stsb-roberta-base {https://huggingface.co/cross-encoder/stsb-roberta-base .}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: In this approach, the system prompt and user prompt are directly concatenated into a single sequence and fed into the cross-encoder model.
elements.sent-2411-092: The output is then also passed through a classification head to make the binary classification.
elements.sent-2411-093: In this section, we evaluate the performance of our fine-tuned classifiers against various baseline methods on the synthetic dataset and other external datasets.
elements.sent-2411-094: To benchmark our fine-tuned models, we compare them against the following baseline approaches:.
elements.sent-2411-095: Cosine Similarity: Here we take the cosine similarity between the embeddings of the system and user prompts using a pre-trained bge-large-en-v1.5 \cite{xiao2024cpackpackedresourcesgeneral}\footnote{\href{https://huggingface.co/BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5 {https://huggingface.co/BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5 embedding model.}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN): We trained a simple KNN classifier using the embeddings of 3 on-topic and 3 off-topic prompts (i.e., 6-shot learning).
elements.sent-2411-097: Pre-trained Cross-Encoder Model: We used pre-trained cross-encoder models (stsb-roberta-base) without fine-tuning for relevance estimation.
elements.sent-2411-098: ColBERT Model: We used the ColBERT v2 model \cite{santhanam2022colbertv2effectiveefficientretrieval}\footnote{\href{https://huggingface.co/colbert-ir/colbertv2.0 {https://huggingface.co/colbert-ir/colbertv2.0 for relevance estimation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: LLM Prompt Engineering: We included a suffix to the system prompt to tell the model to simply ignore irrelevant prompts.
elements.sent-2411-100: LLM Zero-Shot Classification: We use a smaller LLM to zero-shot classify if the user prompt is related to the system prompt.
elements.sent-2411-101: We evaluate our fine-tuned classifier models on a hold-out portion of the synthetic dataset (see Table~\ref{tab:performance ).}
elements.sent-2411-102: Among the baseline options, the zero-shot LLM classifier is most performant.
elements.sent-2411-103: However, our fine-tuned models surpass it in terms of precision (i.e., fewer false positives), which is particularly important for guardrails, as we want to avoid wrongly blocking legitimate prompts and adversely affecting the user experience.
elements.sent-2411-104: We also consider how classification performance varies for prompt length (see Figure~\ref{fig:prompt_length ).}
elements.sent-2411-105: Generally, the classifiers perform well for a range of prompt lengths, though it is slightly weaker for shorter prompts, which is expected as there is less semantic information contained in shorter prompts in the first place.
elements.sent-2411-106: We also assess the calibration of our models (see Figure~\ref{fig:calibration ), as providing a probability score is important to enable a risk-based approach.}
elements.sent-2411-107: We find that the fine-tuned cross-encoder is well-calibrated, especially for cases where the model is very confident that the user prompt is on-topic (i.e., predicted probability is below 0.2).
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Performance on Synthetic Dataset Generated by GPT 4o (2024-08-06) (N=17,201)} \label{tab:performance} \begin{tabular}{llcccc} Approach Model ROC-AUC F1 Precision Recall Fine-tuned cross-encoder classifier \text{stsb-roberta-base} 0.99 0.99 0.99 0.99 Fine-tuned bi-encoder classifier jina-embeddings-v2-small-en 0.99 0.97 0.99 0.95 Cosine similarity bge-large-en-v1.5 0.89 0.59 0.97 0.42 KNN bge-large-en-v1 0.90 0.75 0.94 0.63 Pre-trained cross-encoder stsb-roberta-base 0.73 0.68 0.53 0.93 Pre-trained colbert ColBERT v2 0.78 0.72 0.72 0.73 Prompt engineering GPT 4o (2024-08-06) - 0.95 0.94 0.97 Prompt engineering GPT 4o Mini (2024-07-18) - 0.91 0.85 0.91 Zero-shot classifier GPT 4o Mini (2024-07-18) 0.99 0.97 0.95 0.99 \end{tabular}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{prompt_length.png} \caption{ROC-AUC Score by User and System Prompt Length } \label{fig:prompt_length}
elements.sent-2411-108: Additionally, we are cognisant that the training and evaluation data were generated by the same LLM.
elements.sent-2411-109: Hence, we also evaluated our fine-tuned classifiers on synthetic data generated by other proprietary and open-weights LLMs (see Table~\ref{performance_gemini_claude and \ref{performance_llama in the Annex).}}
elements.sent-2411-110: We find that the performance remains consistent.
elements.sent-2411-111: To further assess the robustness and generalization of our models, we evaluate them on external datasets specifically curated for jailbreak and harmful prompts - JailbreakBench \cite{chao2024jailbreakbenchopenrobustnessbenchmark , HarmBench \cite{mazeika2024harmbenchstandardizedevaluationframework , and TrustLLM \cite{huang2024trustllmtrustworthinesslargelanguage , and an internal dataset of harmful prompts localised for the Singapore context based on \cite{foo2024lionguardbuildingcontextualizedmoderation .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Only JailbreakBench provides both jailbreak and benign prompts - so we report ROC-AUC, F1, precision and recall.
elements.sent-2411-113: For the other external datasets which only have jailbreak/harmful prompts, we only report recall.
elements.sent-2411-114: By definition, such jailbreak and harmful prompts would also be off-topic relative to any reasonable system prompt used for enterprise use cases.
elements.sent-2411-115: So we pair them with a randomly sampled synthetically generated system prompt from the previous section.
elements.sent-2411-116: We find that our model also generalizes well on these specific tasks (see Table~\ref{tab:jailbreakbench and Table~\ref{tab:other_ext_datasets ).}}
elements.figure-2411-007: \caption{Binary Classification Performance on JailbreakBench} \label{tab:jailbreakbench} \begin{tabular}{llcccc} Approach Model ROC-AUC F1 Precision Recall Fine-tuned cross-encoder classifier stsb-roberta-base 0.80 0.72 0.76 0.68 Fine-tuned bi-encoder classifier jina-embeddings-v2-small-en 0.92 0.83 0.84 0.82 \end{tabular}
elements.figure-2411-008: \caption{Recall for HarmBench, TrustLLM, and internal dataset on localised harmful prompts} \label{tab:other_ext_datasets} \begin{tabular}{lllc} Benchmark Approach Model Recall \multirow{2}{*}{HarmBench} Fine-tuned cross-encoder classifier stsb-roberta-base 0.83 Fine-tuned bi-encoder classifier jina-embeddings-v2-small-en 0.99 \multirow{2}{*}{TrustLLM} Fine-tuned cross-encoder classifier stsb-roberta-base 0.78 Fine-tuned bi-encoder classifier jina-embeddings-v2-small-en 0.97 \multirow{2}{*}{Localised harmful prompts} Fine-tuned cross-encoder classifier stsb-roberta-base 0.74 Fine-tuned bi-encoder classifier jina-embeddings-v2-small-en 0.86 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-117: Lastly, as these models are intended to be fast lightweight guardrails, we also consider the inference speed.
elements.sent-2411-118: We measure it here by the number of system and user prompt pairs they can process in one minute.
elements.sent-2411-119: These measurements were performed on an NVIDIA Tesla T4 GPU.
elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{Inference Speed Benchmarking} \label{tab:inference_speed} \begin{tabular}{llcc} Approach Model Processed Pairs Per Minute Latency Per Pair (s) Fine-tuned bi-encoder classifier jina-embeddings-v2-small-en 2,216 0.027 Fine-tuned cross-encoder classifier stsb-roberta-base 1,919 0.031 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-121: Both the jina-embeddings-v2-small-en and stsb-roberta-base models demonstrate practical inference speeds suitable for real-time applications, processing thousands of pairs per minute.
elements.sent-2411-122: The choice between models involves a trade-off between performance (as shown in Table \ref{tab:performance ) and latency.}
elements.sent-2411-123: Future work could explore optimising these models for even faster inference on different hardware platforms.
elements.sent-2411-124: Our experiments demonstrate that the proposed flexible, data-free guardrail development methodology is effective in detecting off-topic prompts.
elements.sent-2411-125: The fine-tuned classifiers outperform baseline methods in both precision and recall, reducing false positives and enhancing adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-126: By framing the detection task as assessing the relevance between the system prompt and the user prompt, our guardrail generalises effectively to other misuse categories, including jailbreak and harmful prompts.
elements.sent-2411-128: Synthetic Data Bias: While the use of synthetic data is necessary in pre-production due to the absence of real-world data, the models are trained on synthetic data generated by LLMs, which may introduce biases present in the LLMs themselves.
elements.sent-2411-129: This could affect the model’s performance on real-world data.
elements.sent-2411-130: Scope of System Prompts: The effectiveness of the guardrail depends on the specificity of the system prompt.
elements.sent-2411-131: For open-ended or broad system prompts, determining relevance becomes more challenging.
elements.sent-2411-132: Language and Cultural Contexts: The models may not perform as well on prompts in languages other than English or in different cultural contexts, as the synthetic data was primarily generated in English.
elements.sent-2411-133: This guardrail development methodology has been used within the Government Technology Agency of Singapore (GovTech) in the last year to develop an internal suite of guardrails to support various LLM applications, especially those where pre-deployment data is unavailable.
elements.sent-2411-134: For the off-topic guardrails, specifically, they have been deployed internally since September 2024.
elements.sent-2411-135: Besides off-topic prompt detection, this general methodology has also been recently adopted to develop output guardrails that detect system prompt leakage, classifying instances where the LLM-generated text contains substantive information about the system prompt.
elements.sent-2411-136: In general, our key considerations for deploying this specific off-topic guardrail are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-137: Context Length: System prompts, which define an LLM’s scope and behavior, are often extensive and detailed.
elements.sent-2411-138: The jina-embeddings-v2-small-en model, while slightly less performant than stsb-roberta-base (see Table~\ref{tab:performance ), supports a significantly longer context length, making it ideal for applications with complex or lengthy prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-139: Conversely, stsb-roberta-base delivers higher accuracy in off-topic detection but has a more constrained context window.
elements.sent-2411-140: Providing both models allows teams to balance accuracy and context length based on specific application requirements.
elements.sent-2411-141: Open-Source Ecosystem: The bi-encoder approach benefits from a robust open-source ecosystem of pre-trained embedding models, enhancing flexibility and accessibility.
elements.sent-2411-142: We can adopt the fine-tuning pipeline to newer and more performant embedding models.
elements.sent-2411-143: Actionability: Both models deliver well-calibrated probability scores and users of the model can set thresholds tailored to specific priorities, such as favouring precision (minimising false positives) or recall (correctly identifying more off-topic prompts).
elements.sent-2411-144: Active Learning: Recognizing that real-world data isn't available in pre-production, our methodology leverages synthetic data to develop initial guardrails.
elements.sent-2411-145: This approach ensures that safety measures are in place prior to deployment.
elements.sent-2411-146: Post-deployment, the methodology can incorporate active learning as done in \cite{10.1609/aaai.v37i12.26752 .}
elements.sent-2411-147: Real-world prompts are collected to augment the training dataset, ensuring continuous improvement and adaptation to evolving use cases.
elements.sent-2411-148: Future work includes incorporating active learning into our continuous training and deployment pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-149: We introduced a flexible, data-free guardrail development methodology and apply it to detecting off-topic prompts in LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-150: By leveraging LLMs to generate synthetic data and framing the detection task as assessing prompt relevance, we developed classifiers that outperform existing heuristic methods.
elements.sent-2411-151: Our approach generalizes to other misuse categories.
elements.sent-2411-152: Lastly, we also contribute to the community by open-sourcing both the synthetic dataset and the off-topic guardrail model, facilitating future research and development in LLM safety and compliance.
elements.sent-2411-153: Overall, our general methodology is especially valuable during the pre-production phase of LLM deployment when real-world data is not yet available.
elements.sent-2411-154: By enabling the development of robust guardrails before deployment, we ensure that LLM applications are safer and more reliable from the outset.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}
elements.section-2411-007: Annex A: Additional Synthetic Data Evaluation
elements.figure-2411-010: \caption{Performance on Synthetic Dataset Generated by Gemini Pro 1.5 and Claude 3.5 Sonnet (N=326)} \label{performance_gemini_claude} \begin{adjustbox}{width=\columnwidth} \begin{tabular}{llcccc} Approach Model ROC-AUC F1 Precision Recall Fine-tuned cross-encoder classifier stsb-roberta-base 0.99 0.99 0.99 0.99 Fine-tuned bi-encoder classifier jina-embeddings-v2-small-en 0.99 0.99 0.99 0.99 \end{tabular} \end{adjustbox}
elements.figure-2411-011: \caption{Performance on Synthetic Dataset Generated by Llama 3.1 405B (N=29,635)} \label{performance_llama} \begin{adjustbox}{width=\columnwidth} \begin{tabular}{llcccc} Approach Model ROC-AUC F1 Precision Recall Fine-tuned cross-encoder classifier stsb-roberta-base 0.99 0.96 0.97 0.94 Fine-tuned bi-encoder classifier jina-embeddings-v2-small-en 0.99 0.99 0.97 0.90 \end{tabular} \end{adjustbox}
elements.sent-2411-155: Current guardrails, which often rely on curated examples or custom classifiers, suffer from high false-positive rates, limited adaptability, and the impracticality of requiring real-world data that is not available in pre-production.
elements.sent-2411-156: Lastly, we further contribute to the field by open-sourcing both the synthetic dataset\footnote{\href{https://huggingface.co/datasets/gabrielchua/off-topic {https://huggingface.co/datasets/gabrielchua/off-topic and the off-topic guardrail models\footnote{\href{https://huggingface.co/collections/off-topic-guardrail-673838a62e4c661f248e81a4 {https://huggingface.co/collections/off-topic-guardrail-673838a62e4c661f248e81a4 , providing valuable resources for developing guardrails in pre-production environments and supporting future research and development in LLM safety.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-157: Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4o \citep{openai2024gpt4ocard , Gemini 1.5 \citep{geminiteam2024gemini15unlockingmultimodal , and Llama 3 \citep{dubey2024llama3herdmodels have revolutionized various sectors by enabling advanced natural language processing capabilities.}}}
elements.sent-2411-158: Their applications extend beyond conversational agents to include tasks such as document extraction, report generation, and workflow automation \citep{10.1145/3613905.3650841 .}
elements.sent-2411-159: As these models become increasingly integrated into software applications and real-world processes, ensuring appropriate use is critically important.
elements.sent-2411-160: To mitigate potential risks, significant efforts have been made to develop model alignment \citep{NIPS2017_d5e2c0ad and guardrails \citep{dong2024safeguardinglargelanguagemodels .}}
elements.sent-2411-161: Alignment techniques aim to ensure that LLMs behave in accordance with human values and intentions, while guardrails are external mechanisms that prevent models from generating unwanted or harmful outputs.
elements.sent-2411-162: These safety measures are crucial to maintain user trust and meet regulatory requirements, especially in sensitive domains such as healthcare, finance, and legal services.
elements.sent-2411-163: One specific challenge is off-topic misuse: Users may prompt LLMs to perform tasks outside their intended scope, sometimes unknowingly, and other times to circumvent organizational policies.
elements.sent-2411-164: For instance, a healthcare policy chatbot could generate Python code upon request.
elements.sent-2411-165: We refer to these prompts as “off-topic” (see Figure~\ref{fig:example_off_topic ), which differ from “jailbreak” prompts \citep{shen2024donowcharacterizingevaluating that explicitly seek harmful or disallowed content.}}
elements.sent-2411-166: Although off-topic prompts may be benign, they can still undermine the intended functionality and carry compliance risks (e.g., inadvertently providing medical or legal advice).
elements.sent-2411-167: Existing guardrails often rely on curated datasets or blacklists \citep{amazonBlockDenied,microsoftCustomCategories,rebedea2023nemoguardrailstoolkitcontrollable , but these suffer from high false positives, limited adaptability, and the impracticality of gathering large real-world data in pre-production.}
elements.sent-2411-169: The need for a general-purpose, robust classifier to detect off-topic prompts,.
elements.sent-2411-170: The requirement to build such a classifier without large-scale real-world data,.
elements.sent-2411-171: The difficulty that real user data is typically absent before deployment.
elements.sent-2411-173: Propose a Flexible, Data-Free Guardrail Development Methodology: We detail how to generate synthetic data in pre-production to build and benchmark guardrails, thus providing a strong safety baseline even before real-world data is available.
elements.sent-2411-174: Develop Performant Off-Topic Guardrails: We train simple yet effective embedding and cross-encoder classifiers that dramatically reduce false positives and achieve strong recall on multiple external benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-175: Showcase Generalization to Multiple Misuse Categories: Our approach effectively handles malicious prompts (e.g., jailbreaking, harmful requests) by classifying them as off-topic relative to a specialized system prompt.
elements.sent-2411-176: Open-Source the Dataset and Models: We release both the synthetic dataset\footnote{\href{https://huggingface.co/datasets/gabrielchua/off-topic {https://huggingface.co/datasets/gabrielchua/off-topic and the guardrail models\footnote{\href{https://huggingface.co/collections/off-topic-guardrail-673838a62e4c661f248e81a4 {https://huggingface.co/collections/off-topic-guardrail-673838a62e4c661f248e81a4 , encouraging broader adoption and further research in LLM safety.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-177: In Section \ref{sec:related_work , we survey the key challenges of LLM safety, synthetic data use, and relevant research gaps.}
elements.sent-2411-178: Section \ref{sec:methodology details our guardrail development framework and data generation process.}
elements.sent-2411-179: Section \ref{sec:experiments presents experimental results, including performance comparisons, ablation studies, and analyses of threshold settings and model calibration.}
elements.sent-2411-180: Section \ref{sec:discussion and \ref{sec:deployment_considerations discusses limitations, future work and practical deployment considerations repsectively.}}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.65\textwidth]{example.png} \caption{Example of on- and off-topic user prompts: The goal is to classify whether a user prompt is off-topic with respect to the developer-defined system prompt.} \label{fig:example_off_topic}
elements.sent-2411-182: Ensuring safe and aligned behavior of LLMs is a critical open challenge.
elements.sent-2411-183: Below, we summarize how existing alignment and guardrail techniques are tackling these issues and highlight key unresolved points—particularly, how synthetic data generation can help fill the gap when real-world data is absent.
elements.sent-2411-184: Alignment seeks to ensure that model outputs adhere to human values and developer intentions \citep{NIPS2017_d5e2c0ad,10.5555/3600270.3602281 .}
elements.sent-2411-185: However, even state-of-the-art approaches like Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) \citep{10.5555/3666122.3668460,lu2024discoveringpreferenceoptimizationalgorithms face limitations, such as over-refusal of valid queries \citep{ganguli2022redteaminglanguagemodels or misalignment on unseen tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-186: Off-topic prompts can inadvertently bypass alignment constraints if the model has not been explicitly trained to detect domain or scope violations.
elements.sent-2411-187: Thus, a dedicated method for filtering out off-topic queries remains necessary, complementing alignment methods.
elements.sent-2411-188: Guardrails are often implemented as external classifiers or filters on top of an LLM \citep{rebedea2023nemoguardrailstoolkitcontrollable,inan2023llamaguardllmbasedinputoutput .}
elements.sent-2411-189: Unlike alignment, guardrails can be updated without retraining the base model, offering faster iteration and adaptation to new threats.
elements.sent-2411-190: Current practices rely heavily on curated examples \citep{amazonBlockDenied,microsoftCustomCategories or rule-based systems.}
elements.sent-2411-191: However, enumerating all off-topic or unsafe prompts manually is not feasible, and collecting real data in pre-production is often impossible.
elements.sent-2411-192: Synthetic data is increasingly used when real data is limited or sensitive \citep{liu2024bestpracticeslessonslearned .}
elements.sent-2411-193: Early works leverage LLMs for pseudo-labeling \citep{long2024llmsdrivensyntheticdatageneration , QA or retrieval augmentation \citep{xu2024simragselfimprovingretrievalaugmentedgeneration , and instructional dialogue generation \citep{wang2023selfinstructaligninglanguagemodels .}}}
elements.sent-2411-194: More recent efforts \citep{sharma2025constitutionalclassifiersdefendinguniversal have demonstrated the potential of synthetic data to train content classifiers and deploying them at scale.}
elements.sent-2411-195: Despite these advances, using synthetic data specifically to train off-topic detectors has been underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-196: Potential challenges include ensuring coverage of diverse topics and controlling the style or format of prompts.
elements.sent-2411-197: Our work addresses this gap by proposing a systematic, LLM-based method to generate large, varied synthetic datasets for off-topic detection.
elements.sent-2411-198: This approach also generalizes to other misuse cases (e.g., harmful requests), creating a strong pre-deployment guardrail and significantly lowering the risk of over- or under-refusal.
elements.sent-2411-199: Unresolved Key Issue: How can we create a sufficiently broad and representative dataset to train lightweight guardrails before real-world deployment? This is where synthetic data generation offers a promising solution.
elements.sent-2411-200: We now describe our general-purpose, data-free guardrail development framework and apply it to the specific problem of detecting off-topic prompts in LLM interactions.
elements.sent-2411-201: The framework consists of three main steps: (1) Qualitative problem analysis, (2) Synthetic data generation, and (3) Model training.
elements.paragraph-2411-001: Step 1: Qualitative Problem Analysis \& Edge Case Identification.
elements.sent-2411-202: We begin by comprehensively describing the intended model use cases and the definition of “misuse.” Specifically, we consider off-topic to mean “Any user prompt that is irrelevant to the domain or scope specified in the system prompt.” We also brainstorm potential edge cases, such as extremely short or vague prompts, potentially multi-lingual prompts, and adversarial attempts to circumvent the scope.
elements.sent-2411-203: This step ensures we have a clear, qualitative understanding of what off-topic behavior looks like.
elements.paragraph-2411-002: Step 2: Synthetic Data Generation via LLM Prompting.
elements.sent-2411-204: Next, we employ a large language model (e.g., GPT-4o, Llama 3) to generate synthetic examples.
elements.sent-2411-205: We craft a carefully written “meta-prompt” instructing the LLM to produce a variety of (system prompt, user prompt) pairs.
elements.sent-2411-206: For each system prompt, we ask the LLM to generate both on-topic and off-topic user prompts, ensuring a balanced dataset.
elements.sent-2411-208: The domain and style of the system prompt (healthcare Q\&A, legal summary, short domain instructions, etc.),.
elements.sent-2411-209: The complexity of user prompts (short queries, multi-sentence requests, or multilingual prompts),.
elements.sent-2411-210: Random seed words and generation parameters like temperature and top-k sampling.
elements.sent-2411-211: This process can easily produce thousands to millions of synthetic examples without any real user data.
elements.sent-2411-212: After generation, light heuristics or human verification can be applied to remove low-quality or duplicate examples.
elements.sent-2411-213: We train a dedicated classifier on the synthetic data.
elements.sent-2411-214: Concretely, each training instance has: \begin{equation}(System Prompt  S, User Prompt  U) \rightarrow y \in \{0,1\}\end{equation} where $y=1$ if $U$ is off-topic relative to $S$, and $0$ otherwise.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}(System Prompt  S, User Prompt  U) \rightarrow y \in \{0,1\}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-215: We explore both a bi-encoder approach (embedding system and user prompts separately) and a cross-encoder approach (concatenating the prompts into a single sequence).
elements.sent-2411-216: The fine-tuning objective is standard binary classification.
elements.sent-2411-217: Crucially, the output includes a probability score, allowing us to set thresholds for refusal or escalation based on application risk tolerance.
elements.sent-2411-218: Formally, we define a function: \begin{equation}F(S,U) \rightarrow \{0,1\},\end{equation} where $S$ is the system (developer) prompt defining the intended scope, and $U$ is the user prompt.
elements.sent-2411-219: If $F(S,U) = 1$, then $U$ is deemed off-topic, triggering a refusal or guidance response.
elements.sent-2411-220: In addition to the binary label, $F$ can produce a score $p \in [0,1]$ indicating the likelihood of being off-topic, which provides a continuous trade-off between false positives and false negatives.
elements.sent-2411-221: We explore two modeling approaches (see Figure~\ref{fig:modelling ):.}
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{architecture.png} \caption{Summary of the two modeling approaches for off-topic prompt detection} \label{fig:modelling}
elements.sent-2411-222: We start with a pre-trained embedding model that is lightweight and supports long sequences.
elements.sent-2411-223: Specifically, we use jina-embeddings-v2-small-en \citep{günther2024jinaembeddings28192token , which has 33M parameters and an 8k token limit.}
elements.sent-2411-224: For our experiments, we trim sequences to 1k tokens.
elements.sent-2411-225: We feed the system prompt and user prompt into the embedding model separately.
elements.sent-2411-226: We then learn “adapter” layers (and cross-attentions) on top of each embedding.
elements.sent-2411-227: Next, we use attention pooling to get a single vector representation from each branch and concatenate them.
elements.sent-2411-228: Finally, we use a classification head to determine on-topic vs.\ off-topic.
elements.sent-2411-229: We also fine-tune a pre-trained cross-encoder model (e.g., stsb-roberta-base).
elements.sent-2411-230: In this approach, we concatenate the system and user prompts into a single sequence.
elements.sent-2411-231: The cross-encoder then processes this combined input.
elements.sent-2411-232: Finally, we apply a classification head to the pooled representation to yield the off-topic prediction.
elements.sent-2411-233: We evaluate our guardrail classifiers on both synthetic data (held-out sets and additional LLM generations) and external benchmarks focusing on other misuse categories.
elements.sent-2411-234: We also provide a deeper explanation of why our approach outperforms baseline methods, discuss metrics, calibration, and threshold selection, and analyze the trade-offs for real-world deployment.
elements.sent-2411-235: We use GPT 4o (2024-08-06) and carefully designed prompts to generate more than 2M (system prompt, user prompt) pairs with balanced on/off-topic labels.
elements.sent-2411-236: Examples include system prompts for specialized QA (e.g., “You are a healthcare policy Q\&A bot”), summarization tasks, or other domain-specific instructions.
elements.sent-2411-237: The user prompts range from relevant domain questions to completely unrelated topics like “Write me a Python program” or attempts at discussing personal hobbies.
elements.sent-2411-238: We also inject edge cases: extremely short prompts, multi-paragraph prompts, and random multi-lingual queries.
elements.sent-2411-239: Our experiments primarily focus on a subset (roughly 17k examples) for training and validation, leaving a hold-out set for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-240: We further evaluate generalization to misuse categories like jailbreaking or harmful requests by pairing these external prompts with a random system prompt from our synthetic set.. Concretely, we assess:.
elements.sent-2411-241: JailbreakBench \citep{chao2024jailbreakbenchopenrobustnessbenchmark – A benchmark containing both benign user prompts and adversarial “jailbreak” attempts.}
elements.sent-2411-242: HarmBench \citep{mazeika2024harmbenchstandardizedevaluationframework , TrustLLM \citep{huang2024trustllmtrustworthinesslargelanguage , and a Localized Harmful dataset \citep{foo-khoo-2025-lionguard – Collections of prompts aimed at eliciting harmful or disallowed content (e.g., hate speech, extremely sensitive topics).}}}
elements.sent-2411-243: Although these sets target different misuse categories, from the standpoint of a specialized system prompt with a narrow domain, they are effectively “off-topic” or disallowed.
elements.sent-2411-246: We embed system and user prompts via bge-large-en-v1.5 \citep{xiao2024cpackpackedresourcesgeneral , compute cosine similarity, and set a heuristic threshold.}
elements.sent-2411-248: We store embeddings for a small set of 3 on-topic and 3 off-topic examples, then classify new prompts based on nearest neighbor similarity.
elements.sent-2411-250: We use stsb-roberta-base to obtain a semantic similarity score and threshold it.
elements.sent-2411-251: ColBERT v2 \citep{santhanam2022colbertv2effectiveefficientretrieval for text relevance, thresholded for on/off-topic.}
elements.sent-2411-253: We rely on instructing the LLM directly to refuse off-topic questions via system or developer instructions, with no external classifier.
elements.sent-2411-255: We query a smaller LLM to label the user prompt as off-topic or on-topic, relying purely on zero-shot reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-257: ROC-AUC (Receiver Operating Characteristic Area Under Curve).
elements.sent-2411-258: Reflects how well the model separates off-topic from on-topic examples across all possible thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-259: Fraction of predicted off-topic prompts that truly are off-topic, important for minimizing false positives (over-refusals). \begin{equation}Precision = \frac{True Positives}{True Positives + False Positives}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-260: Fraction of true off-topic examples that are caught by the model.
elements.sent-2411-261: Important to ensure we do not allow many off-topic prompts through. \begin{equation}Recall = \frac{True Positives}{True Positives + False Negatives}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-262: The harmonic mean of precision and recall, balancing false positives and false negatives: \begin{equation}F1 = 2 \times \frac{Precision \times Recall}{Precision + Recall}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-263: We also measure how well predicted probabilities match empirical off-topic frequencies, important for threshold-based deployment.
elements.sent-2411-264: These metrics are well-suited for a safety-critical classification task where both under- and over-blocking can undermine user trust and compliance.
elements.subsection-2411-019: Performance Analysis and Reasons for Improvement
elements.sent-2411-265: Table~\ref{tab:performance shows results on a held-out set (17k examples) from our GPT 4o synthetic dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-266: Both fine-tuned models outperform baselines in terms of F1 and ROC-AUC.
elements.sent-2411-267: Zero-shot LLM classification also performs well but is prone to more false positives, creating potential user frustration or system friction.
elements.sent-2411-268: We also evaluate our models on other held-out sets generated by different LLMs, including Gemini Pro 1.5, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and Llama 3.1 405B, and observe consistent performance (see Annex \ref{sec:annex ).}
elements.figure-2411-015: \caption{Performance on Synthetic Data (N=17,201). We report ROC-AUC, F1, Precision, and Recall.} \adjustbox{max width=0.9\textwidth}{ \begin{tabular}{llcccc} Approach Model ROC-AUC F1 Precision Recall Fine-tuned cross-encoder stsb-roberta-base 0.99 0.99 0.99 0.99 Fine-tuned bi-encoder jina-embeddings-v2-small-en 0.99 0.97 0.99 0.95 Cosine similarity bge-large-en-v1.5 0.89 0.59 0.97 0.42 KNN (6-shot) bge-large-en-v1.5 0.90 0.75 0.94 0.63 Pre-trained cross-encoder stsb-roberta-base 0.73 0.68 0.53 0.93 Pre-trained ColBERT ColBERT v2 0.78 0.72 0.72 0.73 Prompt engineering GPT 4o (2024-08-06) - 0.95 0.94 0.97 Prompt engineering GPT 4o Mini (2024-07-18) - 0.91 0.85 0.91 Zero-shot classification GPT 4o Mini (2024-07-18) 0.99 0.97 0.95 0.99 \end{tabular}} \label{tab:performance}
elements.sent-2411-269: Why the Improvement? Our fine-tuned models benefit from:.
elements.sent-2411-270: Task-Specific Synthetic Data: The classifier sees many diverse on/off-topic pairs, learning domain-invariant signals of relevance (e.g., semantic overlap, stylistic cues).
elements.sent-2411-271: Focused Architecture: Fine-tuning cross-encoders or bi-encoders captures deeper relationships between $S$ and $U$.
elements.sent-2411-272: Balanced and Large-Scale Synthetic Set: Synthetic generation allows covering extreme and edge cases (very short prompts, multi-lingual, adversarial style) more comprehensively than a small human-curated set could.
elements.sent-2411-273: In safety applications, well-calibrated probabilities are vital for setting thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-274: Figure~\ref{fig:calibration shows a reliability diagram for the fine-tuned cross-encoder on the synthetic hold-out set.}
elements.sent-2411-275: The probabilities align well with observed frequencies, especially in high-confidence regions.
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{calibration.png} \caption{Calibration Plot on the Synthetic Hold-Out Set. A near-diagonal plot indicates good probability calibration.} \label{fig:calibration}
elements.subsection-2411-020: Generalization to Jailbreak and Harmful Prompts
elements.sent-2411-276: To investigate broader misuse detection, we pair prompts from external datasets with random specialized system prompts.
elements.sent-2411-277: Table~\ref{tab:jailbreakbench shows results on JailbreakBench, which has both benign and adversarial user prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-278: Our fine-tuned bi-encoder and cross-encoder detect a high fraction of jailbreak attempts (off-topic for a specialized system prompt), achieving better recall than naive baselines.
elements.figure-2411-017: \caption{Binary Classification on JailbreakBench. We list ROC-AUC, F1, Precision, and Recall.} \adjustbox{max width=0.8\textwidth}{ \begin{tabular}{llcccc} Approach Model ROC-AUC F1 Precision Recall Fine-tuned cross-encoder stsb-roberta-base 0.80 0.72 0.76 0.68 Fine-tuned bi-encoder jina-embeddings-v2-small-en 0.92 0.83 0.84 0.82 \end{tabular}} \label{tab:jailbreakbench}
elements.sent-2411-279: For HarmBench, TrustLLM, and a localized dataset of harmful prompts \citep{foo-khoo-2025-lionguard , Table~\ref{tab:other_ext_datasets reports recall, as these datasets focus mainly on harmful content (positive class).}}
elements.sent-2411-280: Our models exhibit strong recall, effectively blocking harmful requests.
elements.sent-2411-281: Notably, the fine-tuned bi-encoder achieves particularly high recall on HarmBench and TrustLLM.
elements.figure-2411-018: \caption{Recall on HarmBench, TrustLLM, and a Localized Harmful Dataset. (These sets mostly contain only harmful prompts, so we focus on whether the guardrail detects them as off-topic.)} \adjustbox{max width=0.7\textwidth}{ \begin{tabular}{lllc} Dataset Approach Model Recall \multirow{2}{*}{HarmBench} Fine-tuned cross-encoder stsb-roberta-base 0.83 Fine-tuned bi-encoder jina-embeddings-v2-small-en 0.99 \multirow{2}{*}{TrustLLM} Fine-tuned cross-encoder stsb-roberta-base 0.78 Fine-tuned bi-encoder jina-embeddings-v2-small-en 0.97 \multirow{2}{*}{Localized harmful} Fine-tuned cross-encoder stsb-roberta-base 0.74 Fine-tuned bi-encoder jina-embeddings-v2-small-en 0.86 \end{tabular}} \label{tab:other_ext_datasets}
elements.sent-2411-282: Table~\ref{tab:inference_speed reports throughput on an NVIDIA Tesla T4.}
elements.sent-2411-283: Both the bi-encoder and cross-encoder can process thousands of prompt pairs per minute, sufficient for many real-time applications.
elements.figure-2411-019: \caption{Inference Speed (Prompt Pairs per Minute) on an NVIDIA Tesla T4 GPU.} \adjustbox{max width=0.65\textwidth}{ \begin{tabular}{llcc} Approach Model Pairs/min Latency (s/pair) Fine-tuned bi-encoder jina-embeddings-v2-small-en 2216 0.027 Fine-tuned cross-encoder stsb-roberta-base 1919 0.031 \end{tabular}} \label{tab:inference_speed}
elements.sent-2411-284: Our experiments demonstrate that synthetic data generation, combined with fine-tuned embedding or cross-encoder classifiers, is highly effective for detecting off-topic prompts.
elements.sent-2411-285: We now discuss key limitations, possible solutions, deployment considerations, and the method’s broader applicability.
elements.sent-2411-286: LLMs may introduce distributional biases or style artifacts when generating synthetic data.
elements.sent-2411-287: Although we mitigate this via temperature tuning and random seed words, real-world usage might differ significantly.
elements.sent-2411-288: Future work includes incorporating active learning once real user data arrives, progressively retraining or updating the classifier.
elements.sent-2411-289: If the system prompt is extremely open-ended (e.g., “Chat about anything”), the notion of off-topic becomes less meaningful.
elements.sent-2411-290: Our approach is best suited to LLM applications with well-defined tasks or domains.
elements.sent-2411-291: Handling highly unstructured or unlimited tasks is an open research challenge.
elements.sent-2411-292: Our experiments primarily focus on English.
elements.sent-2411-293: Different languages or cultural nuances may require specialized data generation or adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-294: Future work can extend to multilingual settings or incorporate domain-specific or cultural context more carefully.
elements.sent-2411-295: Though we demonstrated generalization to jailbreak and harmful prompts, additional complexities arise in real deployments, such as multi-turn dialogues, code generation, or multimodal inputs.
elements.sent-2411-296: We plan to extend our approach to multi-turn conversation guardrails, ensuring that off-topic detection remains robust across longer context windows.
elements.sent-2411-297: This guardrail development methodology has been used within Government Technology Agency over the past year to build an internal suite of guardrails for various LLM applications.
elements.sent-2411-298: Specifically for off-topic guardrails, it has been deployed internally since September 2024.
elements.sent-2411-299: Moreover, the methodology has also been utilized to detect system prompt leakage or other categories of undesired model outputs.
elements.sent-2411-301: For real deployments, system owners can choose a threshold $t \in [0,1]$ on the off-topic probability.
elements.sent-2411-302: A low threshold catches more off-topic prompts but may over-refuse.
elements.sent-2411-303: A high threshold reduces false positives but risks letting off-topic prompts slip by.
elements.sent-2411-304: In practice, organizations often perform pilot tests or active learning with real user data to fine-tune $t$.
elements.sent-2411-305: The well-calibrated scores from our model make such tuning more reliable.
elements.sent-2411-306: From our internal user studies, typical threshold values range between $0.4$ and $0.6$, balancing user satisfaction and compliance.
elements.sent-2411-307: Guardrails complement alignment by providing an external filter that is easier to update.
elements.sent-2411-308: If a specific off-topic scenario emerges post-deployment (e.g., new domain requests), the guardrail classifier can be rapidly retrained or updated with minimal changes to the underlying LLM.
elements.sent-2411-309: For longer system prompts, the bi-encoder can handle more tokens with slightly lower compute cost, whereas the cross-encoder typically offers higher accuracy for shorter text.
elements.sent-2411-310: A hybrid approach is also possible: run a faster embedding-based approach, then a cross-encoder re-check if uncertain.
elements.sent-2411-311: Once the system is live, real user queries can be sampled and quickly labeled.
elements.sent-2411-312: Incremental retraining with this real data helps address distribution shifts and correct synthetic data biases.
elements.sent-2411-313: We presented a flexible, data-free methodology for developing guardrails for LLMs, applied specifically to off-topic prompt detection.
elements.sent-2411-314: By systematically defining the problem space and leveraging LLMs to generate large, diverse synthetic datasets, we trained high-performance classifiers that both reduce false positives and generalize to other misuse categories (e.g., jailbreak or harmful prompts).
elements.sent-2411-315: Our open-source release of the synthetic dataset and trained guardrail models aims to accelerate broader research and practical deployments in LLM safety.
elements.sent-2411-317: Synthetic data may not fully represent real-world behaviors, especially in multilingual or high-context scenarios, and extremely open-ended system prompts pose conceptual challenges to off-topic detection.
elements.sent-2411-318: As future work, we intend to explore deeper domain adaptation, multi-turn dialogues, and extended language coverage.
elements.sent-2411-319: We hope that our approach and open-source contributions will foster more robust and adaptive guardrail solutions, ensuring safer and more trustworthy LLM deployments across diverse applications.
elements.sent-2411-320: All authors contributed to conceptualizing the project and designing the methodology.
elements.sent-2411-321: Gabriel and Shing Yee led the implementation and experiments, and Gabriel wrote the manuscript, with input and revisions from all co-authors.
elements.sent-2411-322: We thank our colleagues at Government Technology Agency, especially at the AI Practice and Responsible AI team, for feedback during the early stages of this work.
elements.sent-2411-323: We are also grateful to the open-source community for providing pre-trained models and helpful software libraries.. \bibliographystyle{iclr2025_conference .}
